***Rise of the Planet of the Apes*** is a 2011 American [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") [action film](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") directed by [Rupert Wyatt](/wiki/Rupert_Wyatt "Rupert Wyatt") from a screenplay by [Rick Jaffa and Amanda Silver](/wiki/Rick_Jaffa_and_Amanda_Silver "Rick Jaffa and Amanda Silver"). It is a [reboot](/wiki/Reboot_(fiction) "Reboot (fiction)") of the [*Planet of the Apes* film series](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes#Reboot_film_series "Planet of the Apes"), the seventh installment overall and the first in the reboot series.[[7]](#cite_note-Visit-7) The film stars [James Franco](/wiki/James_Franco "James Franco"), [Freida Pinto](/wiki/Freida_Pinto "Freida Pinto"), [John Lithgow](/wiki/John_Lithgow "John Lithgow"), [Brian Cox](/wiki/Brian_Cox_(actor) "Brian Cox (actor)"), [Tom Felton](/wiki/Tom_Felton "Tom Felton"), and [Andy Serkis](/wiki/Andy_Serkis "Andy Serkis").[[3]](#cite_note-BBFC-3)[[6]](#cite_note-AllMovie-6)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Set in 2016, the film follows [Caesar](/wiki/Caesar_(Planet_of_the_Apes) "Caesar (Planet of the Apes)") (Serkis), a genetically enhanced chimpanzee who is first raised by chemist Will Rodman (Franco) before ultimately leading an ape uprising against humanity.

*Rise of the Planet of the Apes* first entered development in 2006 when Jaffa and Silver wrote a [spec script](/wiki/Spec_script "Spec script") and sold it to [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox"), the producers and distributors of the original film series. [Production struggled](/wiki/Development_hell "Development hell") until Franco, Serkis, and Wyatt were hired by late 2009, and [principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") started in July 2010 and finished that September, with filming locations including [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), San Francisco, and [Oahu](/wiki/Oahu "Oahu"). The apes were created using extensive [visual effects](/wiki/Visual_effects "Visual effects") and [motion capture](/wiki/Motion_capture "Motion capture") commissioned by [Weta Digital](/wiki/Weta_Digital "Weta Digital").

*Rise of the Planet of the Apes* was released in the United States on August 5, 2011. The film received positive reviews from critics and was a commercial success, grossing over $481 million worldwide against a budget of $93 million. It received numerous awards and nominations, including nominations for [Best Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects") and [Best Special Visual Effects](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Special_Visual_Effects "BAFTA Award for Best Special Visual Effects") at the [84th Academy Awards](/wiki/84th_Academy_Awards "84th Academy Awards") and [65th British Academy Film Awards](/wiki/65th_British_Academy_Film_Awards "65th British Academy Film Awards"), respectively. It helped relaunch the franchise, and was followed by the sequels *[Dawn of the Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Dawn_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes "Dawn of the Planet of the Apes")* (2014), *[War for the Planet of the Apes](/wiki/War_for_the_Planet_of_the_Apes "War for the Planet of the Apes")* (2017), and *[Kingdom of the Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes "Kingdom of the Planet of the Apes")* (2024).

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In San Francisco, pharmaceutical chemist Will Rodman tests the viral-based drug ALZ-112 on chimpanzees at the biotechnology company Gen-Sys to find a cure for Alzheimer's disease. ALZ-112 is given to a chimpanzee named Bright Eyes, greatly increasing her intelligence. However, during Will's presentation for the drug, Bright Eyes is forced from her cage, goes on a rampage, and is shot to death. Will's superior Steven Jacobs terminates the project and has the chimpanzees euthanized. However, Will's assistant Robert Franklin reveals that the reason for Bright Eyes' rampage was that she had recently given birth. Will reluctantly takes in the infant chimpanzee, eventually naming him [Caesar](/wiki/Caesar_(Planet_of_the_Apes) "Caesar (Planet of the Apes)"). Discovering that Caesar has inherited his mother's intelligence through being exposed to ALZ-112, Will decides to raise him. Three years pass, Caesar becomes highly intelligent; he can communicate through sign language. Will begins a romantic relationship with primatologist, Caroline Aranha. Will and Caroline introduce Caesar to the redwood forest at Muir Woods National Monument so he can roam. Meanwhile, Will treats his father, Charles, with ALZ-112, which seems to restore his cognitive ability.

Five years later, Caesar, now entering adulthood, questions his origins and place in the world. Charles's condition returns as his immune system becomes resistant to ALZ-112. Caesar injures an aggressive neighbor, Douglas Hunsiker, while defending a confused Charles. The local animal control subsequently takes him to an ape shelter. Caesar is tormented by the alpha chimpanzee, Rocket, and the chief guard, Dodge Landon. However, Caesar befriends Maurice, a former circus orangutan who also knows sign language. Caesar learns to unlock his cage, gaining free access to the common area. With the assistance of Buck, a gorilla, Caesar claims the position of alpha. Meanwhile, Jacobs clears the development of a more powerful gaseous version of the drug, the ALZ-113, when Will tells him it can improve intelligence. Will takes the drug home for his father, but Charles declines further treatment and dies overnight. After attempting to test the drug on a bonobo named [Koba](/wiki/Koba_(Planet_of_the_Apes) "Koba (Planet of the Apes)"), Franklin accidentally becomes exposed to ALZ-113 and becomes ill. Despite the risks, Jacobs orders continued trials and Will consequently quits his position.

Attempting to warn Will at his home, Franklin sneezes blood onto Hunsiker and is later discovered dead. Will attempts to reclaim Caesar, but Caesar decides to stay. He escapes and steals the ALZ-113 canisters from Will's house and enhances the intelligence of the other apes in the shelter. When Dodge attempts to get him back into his cage, Caesar reveals he can speak; he fights with Dodge while freeing the apes, inadvertently killing him. The apes flee, releasing Koba and the remaining apes from Gen-Sys, and freeing additional apes from the San Francisco Zoo. A battle ensues as the apes fight their way past a police blockade on the Golden Gate Bridge in an attempt to escape into the redwood forest. To rescue Caesar, Buck sacrifices himself to down a helicopter. Koba pushes the helicopter, with Jacobs still inside, into the sea.

As the apes find their way into the forest, Will finds and warns Caesar that the humans will hunt them, begging him to return home. Caesar hugs him and says he is home. Will accepts Caesar's wishes and the apes embrace their new lifestyle in the forest. Meanwhile, Hunsiker, now infected with ALZ-113, arrives at San Francisco International Airport for his flight. He eventually spreads the virus around the planet, leading to a deadly pandemic.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development and writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development and writing")]

In 2006, screenwriter-producer [Rick Jaffa](/wiki/Rick_Jaffa "Rick Jaffa") was searching for a script idea. As Jaffa searched a newspaper articles [clipping](/wiki/Clipping_(publications) "Clipping (publications)"), one about [pet primates](/wiki/Pet_monkey "Pet monkey") that become troublesome to their owners and not adapted well to the human environment intrigued him. As Jaffa eventually realized it fit the *[Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes "Planet of the Apes")* series, he called his wife and screenwriting partner [Amanda Silver](/wiki/Amanda_Silver "Amanda Silver") to express his ideas of such a chimpanzee eventually starting the ape revolution, and then the couple started developing the character of [Caesar](/wiki/Caesar_(Planet_of_the_Apes) "Caesar (Planet of the Apes)"). Jaffa indicated that "it's a reinvention" and if he had to pick between calling it a prequel or a [reboot](/wiki/Reboot_(fiction) "Reboot (fiction)"), he would say it is a reboot. He went on to say that "we tried really hard to create a story that would stand on its own and yet also pay homage and honor the movies that came before us."[[11]](#cite_note-11) Jaffa and Silver then wrote a script and sold it to [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox"), distributors of the *Apes* film series. The script added other elements which the couple had researched, such as genetic engineering.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Several tributes to specific scenes, characters, and cast and crew from the previous *Apes* film series were added in the script. In particular, Caesar's treatment at the primate sanctuary parallels [Taylor](/wiki/Taylor_(Planet_of_the_Apes)#Taylor "Taylor (Planet of the Apes)")'s treatment as a captive in the original film.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Keegan-14)

[Kathryn Bigelow](/wiki/Kathryn_Bigelow "Kathryn Bigelow"), [Robert Rodriguez](/wiki/Robert_Rodriguez "Robert Rodriguez"), and [Tomas Alfredson](/wiki/Tomas_Alfredson "Tomas Alfredson") were all offered to direct the film, but all of them rejected it.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Director [Rupert Wyatt](/wiki/Rupert_Wyatt "Rupert Wyatt") commented on the originality of the plot, saying, "This is part of the mythology and it should be seen as that. It's not a continuation of the other films; it's an original story. It does satisfy the people who enjoy those films. The point of this film is to achieve that and to bring that fan base into this film exactly like *[Batman Begins](/wiki/Batman_Begins "Batman Begins")*."[[7]](#cite_note-Visit-7) In a 2009 interview, Wyatt said, "We've incorporated elements from *[Conquest of the Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Conquest_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes "Conquest of the Planet of the Apes")*, in terms of how the apes begin to revolt, but this is primarily a prequel to the 1968 film... Caesar is a revolutionary figure who will be talked about by his fellow apes for centuries... This is just the first step in the evolution of the apes, and there's a lot more stories to tell after this. I imagine the next film will be about the all-out war between the apes and humans."[[18]](#cite_note-18) [Mark Bomback](/wiki/Mark_Bomback "Mark Bomback") and [Scott Frank](/wiki/Scott_Frank "Scott Frank") did uncredited rewrites of the script.[[19]](#cite_note-wwtfdotpota-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Filming")]

Filming began on July 27, 2010, in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), British Columbia.[[21]](#cite_note-Felton-21) Filming also happened in San Francisco, California (the primary setting of the film),[[21]](#cite_note-Felton-21) and around [Oahu](/wiki/Oahu "Oahu"), Hawaii, which doubled for the African jungle as the schedule and budget did not allow for location shooting in Africa.[[22]](#cite_note-wyatt-22)

### Visual effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Visual effects")]

As the apes in *Rise* were meant to be real, the producers decided not to use actors in suits. After considering real apes, instead [Weta Digital](/wiki/Weta_Digital "Weta Digital") created the apes digitally in almost every case through [motion capture](/wiki/Motion_capture "Motion capture").[[23]](#cite_note-gen-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Almost 1,500 visual effects shots were previsualized.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Advances in the technology allowed the use of performance capture in an exterior environment, affording the film-makers the freedom to shoot much of the film on location with other actors, as opposed to the confines of a soundstage.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The main breakthrough was a camera that enabled viewing the motion capture dots in daylight, employed mostly for the Golden Gate Bridge battle.[[29]](#cite_note-29) A maximum of six actors could have their movements captured, with larger ape crowds using fully digital animals animated using Weta's move library. The [Golden Gate Bridge](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Bridge "Golden Gate Bridge") set used both a physical set which was extended digitally, and a fully computer-generated model of the bridge that also included the ocean and nearby hills.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

After shooting the actors playing humans interacting with others wearing the motion capture suits, a clean plate was shot with actors for extra reference. Actor-stuntman [Terry Notary](/wiki/Terry_Notary "Terry Notary") guided the actors on realistic ape movement, while Weta studied the chimps in the [Wellington Zoo](/wiki/Wellington_Zoo "Wellington Zoo") for reference. The digital apes also received detailed models with skeletons, muscles and nerve tissue layers for accurate animation. Cast models of apes' heads and limbs helped the texture department replicate skin details such as wrinkles and pores. Given the difference between human and chimpanzee facial muscles, the animators tweaked the performance through a new facial muscle system adding dynamics, ballistics, and [secondary motion](/wiki/Secondary_motion "Secondary motion"). As the silent performance required expressive eyes, a new eye model was made to depict both greater accuracy in muscle movement in and around the eyes, and also tears, [pupil dilation](/wiki/Pupillary_response "Pupillary response"), and light refraction.[[23]](#cite_note-gen-23)[[31]](#cite_note-31) While [Andy Serkis](/wiki/Andy_Serkis "Andy Serkis") was the primary performer for Caesar, as the effects team considered that at times "Andy overcame the character," other motion capture team actors were also used, especially [Devyn Dalton](/wiki/Devyn_Dalton "Devyn Dalton"), whose height matched that of a chimpanzee. Along with that, they used Notary to play Caesar in stunt-filled scenes such as the Golden Gate Bridge scene.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [*Rise of the Planet of the Apes* (soundtrack)](/wiki/Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes_(soundtrack) "Rise of the Planet of the Apes (soundtrack)")

The score for the film was composed by [Patrick Doyle](/wiki/Patrick_Doyle "Patrick Doyle"), performed by the [Hollywood Studio Symphony](/wiki/Hollywood_Studio_Symphony "Hollywood Studio Symphony") and conducted by [James Shearman](/wiki/James_Shearman "James Shearman").[[33]](#cite_note-33) The main concern was to have the music help progress the plot in the scenes without dialogue, for instance, conveying the emotions of Caesar's relationships with Will and Charles. To turn the score into a "driving force that keeps audiences paying attention," Doyle employed an African-American chorus and focused on percussion and "low and deep" orchestra sounds. Doyle collaborated closely with the sound department to make the music complement the sound effects, including writing a recurring theme based on their recording of a chimpanzee.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Box office")]

*Rise of the Planet of the Apes* made its debut in the United States and Canada on roughly 5,400 screens within 3,648 theaters.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The film was projected to gross around $35 million on its opening weekend.[[36]](#cite_note-36) It grossed $19,534,699 on opening day and $54,806,191 in its entire opening weekend, making it #1 for that weekend as well as the fourth-highest-grossing August opening ever.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The film held on to the #1 spot in its second weekend, dropping 49.2%, and grossing $27,832,307.[[38]](#cite_note-38) *Rise of the Planet of the Apes* crossed the $150 million mark in the United States and Canada on its 26th day of release. *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* said that this was quite an accomplishment for the film since the month of August is a difficult time for films to make money.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The film ended its run at the box office on December 15, 2011, with a gross of $176,760,185 in the U.S. and Canada as well as $305,040,864 internationally, for a total of $481,801,049 worldwide.[[4]](#cite_note-BoxOfficeMojo-4)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film holds an 82% approval rating based on 272 reviews, with an average rating of 7.20/10. The website's critics consensus reads, "Led by Rupert Wyatt's stylish direction, some impressive special effects, and a mesmerizing performance by Andy Serkis, *Rise of the Planet of the Apes* breathes unlikely new life into a long-running franchise."[[40]](#cite_note-40) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which uses a weighted average, assigned the film a score of 68 out of 100 based on 39 critics, indicating "generally favorable reviews".[[41]](#cite_note-41) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "A−" on an A+ to F scale.[[42]](#cite_note-CinemaScore-42)

[Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") gave the film 3 out of 4 stars and praised the role of Caesar and [Andy Serkis](/wiki/Andy_Serkis "Andy Serkis") by stating it was a "wonderfully executed character" and "one never knows exactly where the human ends and the effects begin, but Serkis and/or Caesar gives the best performance in the movie."[[43]](#cite_note-43) Giving the film 5 out of 5 stars, Joe Neumaier of *[Daily News](/wiki/Daily_News_(New_York) "Daily News (New York)")* labeled *Rise of the Planet of the Apes* as the summer's best popcorn flick.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Nick Pinkerton of *[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")* wrote, "Caesar's prison conversion to charismatic pan-ape revolutionist is near-silent filmmaking, with simple and precise images illustrating Caesar's General-like divining of personalities and his organization of a group from chaos to order."[[45]](#cite_note-45) Roger Moore of *[Orlando Sentinel](/wiki/Orlando_Sentinel "Orlando Sentinel")* wrote, "Audacious, violent and disquieting, "Rise of the Planet of the Apes" is a summer sequel that's better than it has any right to be." He gave the film 3.5 out of 4 stars.[[46]](#cite_note-46) [Manohla Dargis](/wiki/Manohla_Dargis "Manohla Dargis") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* praised the film by saying, "Precisely the kind of summer diversion that the studios have such a hard time making now. It's good, canny-dumb fun." She also gave it 3.5 out of 4 stars.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

[Peter Travers](/wiki/Peter_Travers "Peter Travers") of *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* noted that the film has mixed "twists lifted from 1972's *[Conquest of the Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Conquest_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes "Conquest of the Planet of the Apes")* and 1999's *[Deep Blue Sea](/wiki/Deep_Blue_Sea_(1999_film) "Deep Blue Sea (1999 film)")*".[[48]](#cite_note-48)

## Sequels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rise_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sequels")]

Main article: [Planet of the Apes § Reboot film series](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes#Reboot_film_series "Planet of the Apes")

Regarding the story setting up possible sequels, director [Rupert Wyatt](/wiki/Rupert_Wyatt "Rupert Wyatt") commented: "I think we're ending with certain questions, which is quite exciting. To me, I can think of all sorts of sequels to this film, but this is just the beginning."[[62]](#cite_note-62) Screenwriter and producer Rick Jaffa also stated that *Rise of the Planet of the Apes* would feature several clues as to future sequels: "I hope that we're building a platform for future films. We're trying to plant a lot of the seeds for a lot of the things you are talking about in terms of the different apes and so forth."[[7]](#cite_note-Visit-7)

On May 31, 2012, 20th Century Fox announced that the sequel would be named *[Dawn of the Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Dawn_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes "Dawn of the Planet of the Apes")*.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Reports said that Wyatt was leaving the sequel due to his concern that a May 2014 release date would not give him enough time to properly make the film;[[64]](#cite_note-64) he was replaced by *[Cloverfield](/wiki/Cloverfield "Cloverfield")* director [Matt Reeves](/wiki/Matt_Reeves "Matt Reeves").[[65]](#cite_note-65) Jaffa and Silver returned as producers and to pen the screenplay, with rewrites from [Scott Z. Burns](/wiki/Scott_Z._Burns "Scott Z. Burns")[[66]](#cite_note-66) and [Mark Bomback](/wiki/Mark_Bomback "Mark Bomback").[[19]](#cite_note-wwtfdotpota-19)

Taking place ten years after *Rise*, *Dawn* follows Caesar's growing nation of evolved apes. Andy Serkis, Terry Notary and Karin Konoval reprise their roles as Caesar, Rocket and Maurice.[[67]](#cite_note-67) James Franco returned as Will Rodman in a "cameo via video".[[68]](#cite_note-68) *Dawn of the Planet of the Apes* was released July 11, 2014.

On January 6, 2014, 20th Century Fox announced a third installment with Reeves returning to direct and co-write along with Bomback, with a planned July 2016 release.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) In January 2015, Fox delayed the release to July 14, 2017.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) On May 14, 2015, the title was given as *War of the Planet of the Apes*,[[73]](#cite_note-73) later re-titled to *[War for the Planet of the Apes](/wiki/War_for_the_Planet_of_the_Apes "War for the Planet of the Apes")*.

On December 3, 2019, it was reported that [Wes Ball](/wiki/Wes_Ball "Wes Ball") was set to direct a then-untitled *Planet of the Apes* film.[[74]](#cite_note-WessBall-74) In August 2019, it was confirmed that any future installments would take place in the same universe first established in *Rise*.[[75]](#cite_note-75) In September 2022, the film's title was revealed to be *[Kingdom of the Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Planet_of_the_Apes "Kingdom of the Planet of the Apes")*, and was set to release on May 10, 2024.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) The film began production in October 2022.[[79]](#cite_note-79)
